subject: Erie Claim A00007555686 — Hongyan Hang — Partial Denial w/ Reinspection Refusal
primaryAdjuster: Yolanda Morrison (initial field inspector — 'told us there was no damage on the roof at all and at the same time she told us to replace the roof asap')
supervisor: Shana Grove, MBA, AIC, Claims Supervisor / Property Claims (240-267-2057, Shana.Grove@erieinsurance.com)
forwardedBy: Benjamin Salgado (Roof-ER): 'Erie legit just sucks. How tf is there no hail damage to this property. Clearest damage in the entire neighborhood. Denied reinspection.'
denialText: This email is to follow up your request for a re-inspection of your property at the above mentioned address. Although we have the utmost confidence in the investigation conducted by Yolanda Morrison, we have reviewed your claim file documents for potential re-inspection. Sufficient photographs were available for review, and we respectfully decline your request for a physical re-inspection. While some of your soft metals did exhibit hail damage, the shingles did not. The roof did display blistering and granule loss, consistent with wear and tear. A partial denial citing the applicable policy exclusions will be sent to you. ... However, if you feel that our assessment is incorrect, you have the option, at your own expense, to hire a professional engineer or similar expert who is qualified to identify hail damage for an assessment. If you do choose to hire an expert, please have them forward a copy of their detailed report which should include photographs of reported hail damages to my attention for review. We do reserve the right to conduct future inspection ourselves or by an engineer we may hire. Please note your policy contains the following provision found in Section RIGHTS AND DUTIES -- CONDITIONS -- SECTION I. LAWSUITS AGAINST US 'We' may not be sued unless there is full compliance with all the terms of this policy. Suit must be brought within two years after the loss or damage occurs.
homeownerCounter: Hello, I'm Hongyan Zhang. I'm reaching out regarding the recent coverage decision on our roof. After reviewing my contractor's photo report, I have serious concerns that a significant amount of storm damage was not properly acknowledged. There is clearly visible hail damage to multiple components of my roof, including the valley flashing, skylights, chimney flashing, pipe jack flashing, and exhaust caps. Plus several clear hail impacts on my roof that several neighbors of mine have been approved already with the same type of damage. Given that all of these metal components show consistent hail damage, and even your field inspector acknowledged the damage to these areas it is difficult to understand how the roof itself was determined to have no storm-related damage. The pattern and consistency of the impacts strongly suggest otherwise. At this point, I am formally requesting that a different adjuster be assigned to my claim and that a full reinspection be conducted or a supplement to address this damage. I want to ensure that all documented damage is properly evaluated and that nothing is overlooked.
keyDenialLanguage: Sufficient photographs were available for review, and we respectfully decline your request for a physical re-inspection
While some of your soft metals did exhibit hail damage, the shingles did not
The roof did display blistering and granule loss, consistent with wear and tear
you have the option, at your own expense, to hire a professional engineer or similar expert
Suit must be brought within two years after the loss or damage occurs
rooferStrategyNotes: Reese Samala: 'Looks like you used 5/31. Should do a new claim for 4/15.' — KEY INSIGHT: DOL strategic refile when carrier disputes one date. Jay Waseem suggested involving a PUBLIC ADJUSTER right away because damage is significant and HO is fully bought in.
carrierTactic: SPLIT-CLASSIFICATION DENIAL — acknowledge hail damage to soft metals (which is hard to deny) BUT classify shingle damage as wear-and-tear (blistering + granule loss). Decline physical reinspection by citing 'sufficient photographs.' Push burden of proof onto homeowner at their own expense via engineer requirement. Cite 2-year suit-against-us provision early to discourage litigation.
rooferTactic: Homeowner cites NEIGHBORS APPROVED for same damage — pattern-of-approvals argument. Reese suggests filing under different DOL when first DOL is disputed. Internal Roof-ER suggestion: involve PA early for significant damage.
patentMapping: Erie uses Verisk/CoreLogic vendor-based AI (Accurence US10089396, US10546264). The 'blistering + granule loss → wear and tear' classification is straight from the patent's documented decision rules. Vendor AI doesn't distinguish well between thermal blistering and hail-induced granule loss — they exploit this ambiguity in the AI's confidence threshold.
lessonForAnalyzer: Erie split-classification denials (soft metals YES / shingles NO): counter-letter should argue (1) pattern of soft-metal hail damage is impossible without corresponding shingle hits — physics-based argument, (2) neighbor approvals for same storm with identical damage type, (3) granule loss VS blistering distinction (granule loss with displaced asphalt vs thermal blistering pattern), (4) reinspection demand with internal staff adjuster — and if denied, file Virginia/MD state DOI complaint citing inadequate investigation.
